mnemonics.title: DP Properties
mnemonics.content: ROG: Recursion + Optimal substructure + Greedy choice (Wait, not greedy!) → Remember: 'Optimal Overlapping' or 'Optimal Overlap' for the two properties needed - Optimal Substructure and Overlapping Subproblems.

mnemonics.title: Fibonacci Base Cases
mnemonics.content: F(0)=0 is like 'Zero' heroes start small; F(1)=1 is 'One' more than zero. Then ADD your way to success!

mnemonics.title: Catalan Numbers Count
mnemonics.content: Catalan numbers count: BSTs, Parenthesizations, Triangulations, and Path Triangles - think 'BST-PT' or 'Binary Trees Prefer Triangles'.

mnemonics.title: DP vs Greedy vs Divide Conquer
mnemonics.content: DP remembers all paths (memorizes), Greedy chooses one path, Divide breaks and conquers each independently.

acronyms.expansion: Dynamic Programming - 'Don't Panic' because it solves by breaking into subproblems
acronyms.usage: Used when problem has optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems

acronyms.expansion: Recursion + Optimal substructure + Greedy choice property
acronyms.usage: Remember: DP needs R and O, NOT greedy!
Fibonacci sequence starts with 0,1 and each subsequent is sum of previous two

visualTips.visualization: Imagine stairs where each step can be reached by taking 1 step from previous stair or 2 steps from the one before. The number of ways to reach each stair forms Fibonacci sequence.
Each row n contains C(n,0), C(n,1), ..., C(n,n). The triangle is symmetric, and each element is sum of two elements above it. C(n,k) is at position (n,k) in the triangle.
Draw a convex polygon. C₃=5 means there are 5 ways to triangulate a convex hexagon (n+2 sides). C₄=14 means 14 ways to triangulate an octagon.
Think of memo table as a phonebook: before calculating, check if number (solution) already exists; if yes, use it; if no, compute and add to phonebook for future use.

analogies.analogy: DP is like building with LEGO bricks: you solve small pieces (subproblems) first, glue them together, and reuse the completed sections instead of rebuilding them each time.
Memoization is like a student solving previous year papers and storing solutions in a notebook. When a similar question appears, they refer to the notebook instead of solving from scratch.

analogies.concept: Tabulation as Filling Water in Containers
analogies.analogy: Tabulation is like filling water containers in order from smallest to largest - you fill container 1, use it to fill container 2, and so on, never going back.

analogies.concept: Overlapping Subproblems as Shared Homework Answers
analogies.analogy: Multiple classmates (subproblems) asking the same question (subproblem) - instead of answering each separately, the teacher (memoization) writes the answer on the board once for all to see.
